GCE A LEVEL MARKING SCHEME

  PREDICTED PAPER – ADVANCED

           A LEVEL
   ECONOMICS – COMPONENT 1
           A520U10-1
GENERAL MARKING GUIDANCE
Positive marking should be applied: credit what the learner writes, rather than penalising omissions.
The indicative content is not exhaustive; learners do not need to cover all points to reach the top
band. Level-based mark schemes sub-divide the total mark across assessment objectives (AO1
Knowledge & Understanding, AO2 Application, AO3 Analysis, AO4 Evaluation). Learners may
achieve different bands within a single question.
                 SECTION A — ANSWERS

Q    Ans                               Notes / Working

1    C     Solow model: labour and capital growth exhibit diminishing returns in
           steady state; only exogenous TFP (technological progress) sustains
           long-run growth in per-capita GDP.

2    B     A Pigouvian tax set at marginal external cost shifts MPC up to MSC;
           output falls to the socially optimal level where MSB = MSC. The
           externality is 'internalised' — pollution is not eliminated but reduced to
           the socially optimal quantity.

3    C     Under a natural monopoly LRAC is falling, so MC < AC over the
           relevant range. Regulating P = MC means P < AC → the firm makes a
           loss (requires subsidy or different regulatory rule, e.g. average-cost
           pricing).

4    B     At P = £50: Qd = 600 − 4(50) = 400; Qs = −100 + 6(50) = 200. Excess
           demand = 400 − 200 = 200 units. Equilibrium P would be £70 (not
           enforced here).

5    B     Real ER change ≈ Nominal ER change + (π_home − π_foreign) = +1%
           + (6% − 2%) = +5% appreciation. UK goods become relatively more
           expensive abroad.

6    B     LRPC is vertical because in the long run workers correctly anticipate
           inflation and build it into wage demands, so there is no lasting
           output/employment gain from higher inflation (Friedman–Phelps
           expectations-augmented Phillips curve).

7    B     Progressive redistribution narrows the gap between richest and
           poorest quintile shares, flattening the Lorenz curve towards the 45° line
           and lowering the Gini coefficient.

8    A     Pre-tax: 120 − 2Q = 20 + 3Q → Q = 20, P = £80; CS = ½ × (120 − 80)
           × 20 = £400. Post-tax (supply shifts up £10): 120 − 2Q = 30 + 3Q → Q
           = 18, P_c = £84; CS = ½ × (120 − 84) × 18 = £324. Loss of CS = £400
           − £324 = £76.

9    B     Perfect contestability requires zero sunk costs and free entry/exit; even
           with few incumbents, the threat of 'hit-and-run' entry forces firms to
           price at normal profit in the long run (Baumol).

10   B     In listed firms, shareholders (principals) delegate decision-making to
           managers (agents). Asymmetric information and divergent objectives
           (profit vs empire-building, salary, perks) create agency costs.

11   B     At the ZLB, the policy rate is already at (or very close to) 0% and
           cannot be cut further to stimulate demand without unconventional tools
           (QE, forward guidance, negative rates in some jurisdictions).

12   C     In open economies, leakages to imports, partial crowding-out of private
           investment, and offsetting exchange-rate movements all reduce the
           effective multiplier below its theoretical closed-economy value (often
           0.5–1.5 empirically).

13   C     With perfectly inelastic demand, consumers absorb the entire tax;
           quantity demanded is unaffected. Tax revenue = tax × Q is therefore
           maximised (no demand response).

14   B     TOT improvement = export prices rise relative to import prices. With
           volumes unchanged (Marshall-Lerner not binding here since Q doesn't
         respond), the value of exports rises relative to imports → current
         account improves.

15   D   QE works via asset-price, portfolio-rebalancing, exchange-rate and
         signalling channels. It does NOT guarantee increased bank lending to
         SMEs — banks may hold excess reserves rather than lend (a key
         critique of QE post-2008).

16   B   Behavioural economics (Thaler & Sunstein) shows consumers are not
         fully rational; small changes to defaults, framing, or choice architecture
         can produce large behavioural changes without restricting choice —
         the 'libertarian paternalist' approach.

17   D   Commons are rival (one person's use reduces availability for others)
         and non-excludable (cannot prevent access). This combination leads
         to over-exploitation: each user captures the full benefit but only a
         fraction of the cost.

18   C   Real GDP growth = (Nominal growth)/(Deflator growth) − 1 =
         (2184/2000) / (104/100) − 1 = 1.092/1.04 − 1 = 1.05 − 1 = 5.0%.

19   C   Kuznets (1955): inequality first rises as industrialisation draws labour
         from low-productivity agriculture into higher-productivity industry
         (raising dispersion), then falls as wages converge and redistributive
         policies emerge — an inverted-U shape.

20   C   Pre-tax eq: 500 − 10P = 20P − 100 → P = £20, Q = 300. With £6 tax
         on producers, effective supply: Qs = 20(P − 6) − 100 = 20P − 220. New
         eq: 500 − 10P = 20P − 220 → 30P = 720 → P_c = £24, Q = 260.
21.   Using the diagram, explain why price stability is a common feature of   Total
      oligopoly markets.

      AO1: 2 marks                                                             3
      Award 2 marks for good knowledge of the kinked-demand model
      (Sweezy, 1939): rivals match price cuts but ignore price rises,
      producing a demand curve that is elastic above and inelastic below
      P*, with a discontinuity in MR.
      Award 1 mark for limited understanding or incorrect use of the
      diagram.

      AO3: 1 mark
      Award 1 mark for analysis: firms' MC can shift within the MR 'gap'
      without altering profit-maximising output or price, so prices stay
      sticky at P*.

      Indicative content:
      • If one firm RAISES price above P*, rivals do not follow → the
      raising firm loses substantial market share (elastic upper demand
      segment) → TR falls → disincentive to raise price.
      • If one firm CUTS price below P*, rivals match → the firm gains
      little market share (inelastic lower demand segment) → TR falls →
      disincentive to cut price.
      • The discontinuity in MR (the vertical gap A–B) means that for a
      range of MC values the profit-maximising condition MC = MR still
      yields the same output Q* and price P* → price is 'sticky'.
      • This explains the empirical observation that oligopolists tend to
      compete through non-price means (advertising, product
      differentiation) rather than price wars.
22. Consider the extent to which a £50bn expansionary fiscal stimulus would be an effective
policy response. Adapt the diagram as part of your answer. (6 marks)

   Band                 AO1 (2)                        AO3 (2)                        AO4 (2)

      2       2 – Good knowledge             2 – Good analysis               2 – Good evaluation
              Clear grasp of Keynesian       Strong reasoning: in deep       Strong counter-argument:
              AS (flat → upward-sloping      recession, spare capacity       crowding-out risk (even in
              → vertical). AD shifts right   means fiscal stimulus           recession), time lags,
              by k × £50bn due to            raises real GDP with            deficit financing concerns,
              multiplier. Diagram            minimal inflation. Multiplier   multiplier size uncertain
              correctly shows AD₁ →          effect strongest here (k ≈      empirically, Ricardian
              AD₂ along flat portion of      1/(1−MPC)).                     equivalence, political
              AS, with ΔY but no ΔP.                                         economy of reversal.
              Fully credit any reference
              to the multiplier with MPC.

      1         1 – Limited knowledge           1 – Limited analysis           1 – Limited evaluation

      0                    0                               0                              0

Indicative content:
• £50bn of government spending shifts AD to the right. Under the Keynesian multiplier k = 1/(1 −
MPC), if MPC = 0.6, total AD shift ≈ £125bn.
• On the flat portion of Keynesian AS (deep recession, high unemployment, idle capacity), the shift
raises real GDP from Y₁ to Y₂ with no increase in the price level — the Keynesian 'free lunch'.
• Diagram: AD₁ → AD₂; along flat AS, ΔY is large, ΔP is zero. Only as Y approaches full employment
does the price level start rising (as AS becomes upward-sloping/vertical).
For the view that fiscal stimulus is effective:
• In deep recession, monetary policy is often ineffective (ZLB, liquidity trap) → fiscal becomes the only
credible lever (IMF research, 2010s).
• Public investment (infrastructure) can raise LRAS as well as AD, so the stimulus has supply-side
benefits too (non-inflationary growth).
• Automatic stabilisers supplement discretionary policy.
• Historical examples: Roosevelt's New Deal; China's 2008–09 stimulus; US CARES Act (2020).
BUT (evaluation):
• Crowding out: even in recession, bond issuance may raise long-term interest rates and crowd out
private investment (Ricardian equivalence in the extreme).
• Time lags: recognition, decision, implementation and effect lags mean stimulus may arrive as the
economy is already recovering — pro-cyclical.
• Deficit financing worsens national debt; if debt-to-GDP becomes unsustainable, bond markets may
punish the government (UK mini-budget 2022 as recent example).
• Empirical multiplier estimates vary widely (0.5 to 2.5); the true size is uncertain.
• Import leakages in open economies reduce domestic impact.
• Quality of spending matters — malinvestment (politically-driven projects) may not deliver multiplier
effects.
• Allow any other sensible evaluation.
23.   With the aid of a diagram, comment on the likely consequences for            Total
      lithium prices over the next 5 years, referring to the price elasticity of
      supply.

      AO1: 2 marks                                                                  3
      Award 2 marks for good understanding of PES determinants (time
      period, stock availability, spare capacity, factor mobility). Accurate
      diagram showing a steep (inelastic) short-run supply curve with a
      large rightward demand shift producing a large ΔP and small ΔQ.
      Award 1 mark for limited understanding; diagram missing or
      inaccurate.

      AO2: 1 mark
      Award 1 mark for using the stimulus (7–10 year lead times, existing
      mines at capacity, 4× demand) effectively to justify very inelastic
      short-run PES and the resulting sharp price rise.

      Indicative content:
      • PES is very low in the short run because mine expansion takes 7–
      10 years — the time period is shorter than the adjustment window,
      and existing capacity is nearly exhausted.
      • Diagram: near-vertical short-run supply curve. D₁ → D₂ (demand
      quadruples): price rises sharply from P₁ to a much higher P₂, with a
      relatively small increase in quantity.
      • Consequences: battery-input cost inflation; pressure on EV
      manufacturers' margins; incentive for substitution (sodium-ion,
      solid-state batteries); strategic stockpiling; possible export
      restrictions by producing nations.
      • In the long run, PES becomes more elastic as new mines come
      online and recycling scales up, so prices may ease — cobweb-type
      dynamics possible.
24.   Explain the shape of this firm's short-run total cost curve and the       Total
      economic significance of the discontinuity.

      AO1: 2 marks                                                               4
      Award 2 marks for good understanding of fixed vs variable costs,
      returns to a factor, and the significance of 'lumpy' capital investment
      producing a discontinuous (step) cost function.
      Award 1 mark for limited understanding with gaps or confusion.

      AO3: 2 marks
      Award 2 marks for strong chain of reasoning linking each segment
      of the TC curve to underlying economic concepts (division of
      labour, specialisation, integer problem of capital).
      Award 1 mark for underdeveloped analysis.

      Indicative content:
      • 1–99 units (artisan stage): TC rises from fixed cost base.
      Increasing returns to skilled labour produce a gently-rising TC
      curve. At Q=99, TC = £5,000.
      • Jump at Q=100 from £5,000 to £25,000 (£20,000 step): reflects
      the indivisible capital investment required to shift to factory
      production — machinery, premises, tooling. Classic 'lumpy' fixed
      cost.
      • 100–500+ units (factory stage): TC rises gradually again, initially
      at a shallower gradient than the artisan stage (economies of scale
      — machinery spreads fixed cost over higher output), but eventually
      rising as diminishing returns set in.
      • Economic significance: (i) the firm should only invest in the factory
      stage if the expected output justifies the £20,000 extra fixed cost;
      (ii) there is a break-even output above which factory production is
      cheaper per unit than artisan; (iii) the discontinuity explains why
      some firms hover at Q=99 despite unmet demand — the jump in
      TC makes the next unit uneconomic at low output.
25. With reference to the matrix and economic theory, discuss whether the optimal trade
policy is unilateral free trade. (8 marks)

  Band              AO2 (3)                       AO3 (2)                         AO4 (3)

    3      3 – Excellent application                                  3 – Excellent evaluation
           Matrix fully used. Both                                    Strong counter-argument using
           countries' strategies                                      optimum tariff theory, strategic
           analysed. Dominant                                         trade policy, infant industry,
           strategies identified; uses                                Rodrik's 'trilemma', or WTO
           specific growth rates                                      context. Recognises unilateral
           (+2.4, +3.0, −3.5, −4.0).                                  free trade has theoretical
           Notes asymmetry (X                                         appeal but political economy
           developed, Y emerging).                                    constraints.

    2      2 – Good application          2 – Good analysis            2 – Good evaluation
           Matrix used; both             Strong reasoning on          Strong counter-argument about
           strategies discussed with     comparative advantage /      when unilateral free trade may
           some reference to the         gains from trade / welfare   not be optimal. Theory use
           growth rates.                 gains from unilateral        more limited.
                                         liberalisation.

    1        1 – Limited application        1 – Limited analysis          1 – Limited evaluation

    0                   0                            0                               0

The grid assumes that the main line is in favour of unilateral free trade, but the answer is fully
reversible.

Indicative content:
AO2 (matrix interpretation):
• The (Free trade, Free trade) outcome gives the highest joint gains: (+2.4, +3.0). Total world welfare
is maximised here.
• The (Trade war, Trade war) outcome is the worst jointly: (−3.5, −4.0). Mutual destruction.
• Note the asymmetry: Economy X (large developed) loses 3.5% in a trade war; Economy Y
(emerging) loses 4.0% — Y is more exposed because a larger share of its GDP is trade-dependent.
• If country X engages unilaterally in free trade while Y protects: X gets some welfare gains from
lower import prices and consumer surplus, but loses on export market access. Depending on the pay-
offs in the off-diagonal cells (not shown), unilateral free trade may still be better than a trade war.
• Free trade is the Pareto-optimal outcome; trade war is the Nash equilibrium if one-shot (each fears
defection). Repeated play enables free trade through reputation.
AO3 (if in favour of unilateral free trade):
• Consumer welfare: unilateral tariff removal lowers prices, widens choice, raises CS — the classic
argument (Smith, Ricardo).
• Comparative advantage: resource reallocation to most-efficient sectors raises productivity and real
GDP.
• Competitive pressure on domestic firms forces efficiency gains (dynamic/X-efficiency).
• Lower input costs for producers → lower export prices → potentially higher export competitiveness.
• Historical cases: UK's 19th-century Corn Law repeal; Hong Kong's free-port model; Chile's 1970s
unilateral liberalisation.
AO4 (reasons unilateral free trade may NOT be optimal):
• Optimum tariff theory: a large country can improve its TOT by levying a tariff (the tariff revenue +
TOT gain can exceed DWL if foreign PES is inelastic).
• Strategic trade policy (Brander-Spencer): targeted support for industries with increasing returns can
shift rents to domestic firms.
• Infant industry argument (List, Hamilton): protection allows developing-country firms to achieve MES
before competing.
• Employment/transition costs: displaced workers in uncompetitive sectors may not re-skill quickly; the
China Shock literature (Autor et al.) documents durable labour-market damage.
• Strategic/defence industries require protection regardless of comparative advantage.
• Dumping and unfair subsidies by trading partners make unilateral openness vulnerable to predatory
pricing.
• Political economy: losers concentrate, winners dispersed → democratic pressure favours protection
(Olson).
• Rodrik's globalisation trilemma: cannot have hyperglobalisation, democracy and national
sovereignty simultaneously.
• Revenue consideration for developing countries: tariff revenue is easy to collect and funds
infrastructure (columns 5 and 6 of the matrix reflect this via Y's higher WTO bound tariff).
• Allow any other sensible evaluation.
26. With reference to the data above, consider the extent to which the UK's carbon pricing
policies are effective at correcting the market failure of climate change. (8 marks)

  Band                AO2 (3)                  AO3 (2)                         AO4 (3)

    3      3 – Excellent application                              3 – Excellent evaluation
           Quantitative data (48%                                 Strong counter-argument using
           CO₂ fall, 43%                                          government failure, regulatory
           renewables, £40 vs £240                                capture, carbon leakage,
           carbon price, PED                                      distributional concerns, global
           −0.3/−0.7) and textual                                 collective-action problem (1%
           data used together to                                  UK share).
           build argument.

    2      2 – Good application        2 – Good analysis          2 – Good evaluation
           Numerical and textual       Strong reasoning on how    Counter-argument that UK
           data used to support        a Pigouvian-style carbon   carbon pricing may not
           arguments.                  price internalises the     effectively correct the failure;
                                       negative externality       theory use more limited.
                                       (Pigou/Coase/Stern
                                       Review).

    1        1 – Limited application     1 – Limited analysis          1 – Limited evaluation

    0                   0                         0                               0

The grid assumes that the main line is in favour of carbon pricing effectiveness, but the answer is fully
reversible.

Indicative content:
AO2:
• UK emissions down 48% since 1990 — significant decoupling of emissions from growth; renewables
share up from 7% to 43% (a sixfold rise), consistent with carbon pricing working.
• BUT the ETS price of £40/tonne is far below the Climate Change Committee's recommended
shadow price of £240/tonne by 2030 — suggesting the externality is only partially priced.
• Fuel duty frozen since 2011 — in real terms, the carbon price on road transport has fallen,
weakening the behavioural signal. Combined with short-run petrol PED of only −0.3, fuel-duty
effectiveness is limited.
• ETS revenue fell from £5.5bn (2023) to £3.2bn (2024) — carbon price volatility reduces policy
predictability and firm-level investment confidence.
• UK is only ~1% of global emissions; domestic effectiveness limited if major emitters (China, India,
US) do not follow.
AO3 (if effective):
• Classic Pigouvian logic: externality of CO₂ → socially optimal output is where MSB = MSC, achieved
by a tax or tradeable permit equal to the marginal external cost. Internalises the externality.
• Cap-and-trade (ETS) has the advantage of quantity certainty: the cap guarantees emissions
reductions regardless of the price.
• Carbon pricing raises cost of carbon-intensive activities → substitution to renewables/efficiency →
confirmed by 48% decline in emissions and 43% renewables share.
• Dynamic efficiency: persistent carbon price signal incentivises R&D into clean technologies (induced
innovation).
• Long-run PED for petrol (−0.7) is more elastic than short-run (−0.3): price signal effective over time
as consumers adjust vehicles, housing, behaviour.
AO4 (reasons carbon pricing may NOT effectively correct the failure):
• Government failure: the price (£40) is set too low versus the shadow cost (£240), leaving most of
the externality unpriced — MSC still exceeds MPC for most CO₂ emissions.
• Only ~30% of emissions are covered by the UK ETS; large sectors (transport, buildings, agriculture)
face weaker or no carbon signal.
• Carbon leakage: if UK firms face high carbon prices, production relocates to low-carbon-price
jurisdictions — emissions not reduced, only outsourced. UK emissions on a consumption basis have
fallen much less than on a production basis.
• Regressive distributional impact: carbon prices affect low-income households proportionately more
(home heating, petrol are necessities) → political opposition (cf. French 'gilets jaunes', 2018).
• Frozen fuel duty shows political economy constraints — governments reluctant to raise visible costs.
• Climate change is a global public good / collective-action problem: UK's 1% share means unilateral
pricing has limited direct impact on global temperatures. Free-riding is the dominant strategy.
• Regulatory/command approaches (boiler bans, EV mandates) may deliver more certain reductions
than price signals alone.
• Volatility of ETS price (£90 → £40 in one year) undermines long-term investment signals.
• Allow any other sensible evaluation.
27. Using the information above and relevant economic theory, discuss the extent to which
institutional quality is the most fundamental determinant of long-run economic development.
(8 marks)

  Band               AO2 (3)                     AO3 (2)                       AO4 (3)

    3       3 – Excellent application                              3 – Excellent evaluation
            All columns used. Clear                                Strong counter-argument:
            comparisons — e.g.,                                    geography (Diamond, Sachs),
            South vs North Korea                                   culture (Weber), human capital
            (same                                                  (Lucas), luck/initial conditions.
            ethnicity/geography,                                   Engages with
            opposite institutions);                                causality/reverse-causality
            Chile vs Argentina;                                    problem.
            Botswana vs Zimbabwe.
            Rule-of-law index
            integrated.

    2       2 – Good application        2 – Good analysis          2 – Good evaluation
            Data used to support the    Strong chain: inclusive    Alternative determinants of
            argument; at least one      institutions → property    development considered.
            strong natural-experiment   rights → investment →      Theory use more limited.
            comparison.                 growth (Acemoglu-
                                        Robinson).

    1        1 – Limited application       1 – Limited analysis         1 – Limited evaluation

    0                   0                           0                              0

The grid assumes that the main line is in favour of the institutional-quality hypothesis, but the answer
is fully reversible.

Indicative content:
AO2 (data interpretation):
• The Korean natural experiment is decisive: South Korea (HDI ≈ 0.93, inclusive institutions, high rule-
of-law index) vs North Korea (HDI ≈ 0.5, extractive institutions). Same ethnicity, geography, initial
conditions — divergent institutions produced radically different development outcomes.
• Chile (HDI ≈ 0.86, stronger rule of law) vs Argentina (HDI ≈ 0.84, weaker, prone to populist cycles):
similar geography, different policy/institutional trajectories produced a growing gap from the 1970s.
• Botswana (HDI ≈ 0.73, strong institutions post-1966) vs Zimbabwe (HDI ≈ 0.55, catastrophic
institutional collapse under Mugabe): both landlocked, similar natural resource bases — divergent
institutions explain the gap.
• Singapore (HDI ≈ 0.94, world-leading rule of law despite being soft-authoritarian) vs Nigeria (HDI ≈
0.54, oil-rich but institutionally fragile): oil is not a substitute for governance.
• The correlation between rule-of-law index and HDI in the data is strong, consistent with the
institutional-quality hypothesis.
AO3 (if institutional quality IS the fundamental determinant):
• Acemoglu & Robinson (Why Nations Fail, 2012): inclusive institutions (broad property rights,
inclusive political voice, rule of law, constraints on executive power) generate investment, innovation
and growth. Extractive institutions (elite capture, weak property rights) produce stagnation.
• Property rights secure returns to investment (human and physical capital). Without them, the
incentive to accumulate is weak (North, 1990).
• Rule of law reduces transaction costs, expands the reach of markets, and disciplines corruption (De
Soto).
• Inclusive institutions are self-reinforcing: wider participation produces political accountability, which
protects institutions from capture.
• Empirical: Acemoglu-Johnson-Robinson (2001) use settler-mortality as an instrumental variable,
finding a causal effect of institutions on current GDP per capita.
• The 'resource curse' is institutionally conditioned: Norway and Botswana handle resource wealth
well; Venezuela and Nigeria do not — difference is institutional.
AO4 (reasons institutions may NOT be the most fundamental determinant):
• Geography hypothesis (Diamond, Sachs): tropical disease burden, landlocked status, and soil
quality strongly predict development. Africa's challenges partly reflect malaria, tsetse fly, and
remoteness from trade routes.
• Culture/religion (Weber's Protestant work ethic; Putnam's social capital; Greif's cultural beliefs):
values of thrift, trust, and cooperation may be deep drivers of which institutions emerge.
• Human capital (Lucas, 1988; Glaeser et al., 2004): some argue causality runs from education →
institutions rather than the reverse. Educated populations demand better institutions.
• Reverse causality: rich countries can AFFORD better institutions — so the correlation may not be
causal in the direction institutionalists claim.
• Initial conditions and path dependence matter — lucky accidents of history (the Industrial Revolution
in Britain) shape trajectories.
• Dependency theory (Prebisch-Singer, Frank): historical exploitation of the 'periphery' by the 'core'
constrains institutional development. The colonial inheritance of extractive institutions (as A&R
themselves note) is a geographic/historical imposition, not just an institutional choice.
• Washington Consensus critique: institutional reforms imposed externally often failed (e.g. 1990s
Russia privatisation without rule of law); institutions must be home-grown, which qualifies policy
implications.
• China's 1978–present growth under formally extractive (one-party) institutions challenges the strict
inclusive-institution thesis — suggests high-quality 'growth-promoting' institutions can take many
forms.
• In practice, institutions, geography, human capital, culture and luck all interact — isolating 'the most
fundamental' is methodologically contested.
• Allow any other sensible evaluation.
